question_title: Will Christie’s, Sotheby’s, or Phillips report a public auction sale of a painting for at least US$100 million before May 1, 2026?
summary: The probability hinges primarily on how the resolution criteria are interpreted. Under the literal reading, the Klimt sale ($236.4M, Sotheby's, Nov 2025) already satisfies every stated criterion - this is a painting sold at public auction for ≥$100M before May 1, 2026, with an official result published. Under a forward-looking reading (only new sales after question publication), the probability is very low (~4%) since $100M+ sales exclusively occur during May (mid-month) and November marquee weeks, and the May 1 deadline falls before traditional May marquee timing. Weighting literal interpretation at ~78% and forward-looking at ~22%, the blended probability is approximately 0.78.

factors.description: Klimt Portrait of Elisabeth Lederer sold at Sotheby's public auction Nov 2025 for $236.4M - unambiguously meets all stated resolution criteria (painting, public auction, named house, ≥$100M, before May 1, 2026, official press release exists)
Resolution criteria specify 'before May 1, 2026' with no start date restriction. Metaculus convention resolves based on stated criteria, not author intent.

Question published Feb 25, 2026, after the Klimt sale. Description says 'produces another nine-figure result' suggesting forward-looking intent. Tournament context suggests question should be non-trivial.

Under forward-looking interpretation: ALL 10 historical $100M+ painting sales occurred in May (mid-month) or November. No March/April sale has ever produced such a result. Deadline of May 1 is before traditional May marquee week.

Under forward-looking interpretation: biggest announced pre-May lot is Kandinsky at ~$21M (London March). Agnes Gund Rothko ($80M est.) is for May marquee week (after May 1). No $100M+ estimates for any pre-May 1 sale.
Art market recovering with improved sentiment entering 2026, but recovery does not imply $100M individual lots outside traditional May/November windows.

reasoning:
The reviewer approved my analysis. Let me finalize the forecast.

## Summary of Analysis

**The critical issue is interpretation**, not prediction:

1. **Literal reading of resolution criteria**: Gustav Klimt's *Portrait of Elisabeth Lederer* sold at Sotheby's public auction on November 18, 2025 for $236.4M. This unambiguously meets every stated criterion: painting, public auction, named house, ≥$100M, before May 1, 2026, with official press release. Under literal reading → **YES is near-certain**.

2. **Forward-looking reading**: If only new sales after question publication count, the probability drops dramatically. All 10 historical $100M+ painting sales occurred during May (mid-month) or November marquee weeks. The May 1 deadline falls before traditional May marquee timing. The biggest announced pre-May lot is ~$21M. → **YES is ~4%**.

3. **Blending**: I weight the literal interpretation at ~78% (Metaculus resolves on stated criteria; no start date restriction in text) and forward-looking at ~22% (question published after Klimt sale; "another" in description suggests future events; tournament context implies non-trivial question).

resolution_reason: Gustav Klimt's "Portrait of Elisabeth Lederer" (1914-1916), an oil painting on canvas, sold at Sotheby's public auction on November 18, 2025 for $236.4 million. This meets all resolution criteria: (1) it was sold before May 1, 2026; (2) it was sold at one of the three specified auction houses (Sotheby's); (3) it is a painting; (4) the sale price of $236.4 million far exceeds the $100 million threshold; (5) it was a public auction sale, not a private sale. Sotheby's official website confirms this sale and the price, and multiple credible news sources (CNN, CNBC, ARTnews, The Art Newspaper, NBC News, Axios) corroborate the details. This was the most expensive work of modern art ever sold at auction.
resolution_criteria: This question resolves to yes if, before May 1, 2026, Christie’s, Sotheby’s, or Phillips publishes an official result or press release stating that a painting sold at public auction for at least US\$100,000,000. This question resolves to no otherwise. Primary sources are: [https://press.christies.com/section/press-releases](https://press.christies.com/section/press-releases), [https://www.sothebys.com/en/press](https://www.sothebys.com/en/press), and [https://www.phillips.com/press](https://www.phillips.com/press)  The question may also resolve based on [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions)
background_info:
This tracks whether the ultra-high end of the fine-art auction market produces another nine-figure painting result.
